content.problemStatement:
You are playing a computer game. In the current level of the game, you have to fight NNN monsters.

The monsters will appear in order from 111 to NNN, that is, the ithi^{th}ith monster will appear after the (i−1)th(i-1)^{th}(i−1)th monster.

As soon as a monster appears:


You must fight with the newly appeared monster, leaving aside any other monster you were fighting with.
If you manage to kill the monster you are fighting with, you will return to the monster you were fighting before.
If you manage to kill the monster you are fighting with, and you have already fought with all monsters that arrived before it, you will wait for the next monster to appear.
In the middle of the game, you took a break. You told your friend you had successfully defeated the KthK^{th}Kth monster before taking the break.

Your friend wonders the number of different possible valid orders to defeat the remaining monsters. As the answer can be large, output it modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the total number of monsters, and the KthK^{th}Kth monster, which you have defeated before taking a break, respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line number of possible valid orders of defeating the remaining monsters modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 5⋅1035 \cdot 10^35⋅103.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The second monster has been defeated. So we will find all the possible cases of defeating the remaining monsters.

Case 111: Monster 111 and 333 were already defeated. So the order of defeating the monsters becomes {}\{\}{}.
Case 222: Monster 111 was defeated, but monster 333 didn't appear till now. So, the order of defeating the monsters becomes {3}\{3\}{3}.
Case 333: Monster 333 was defeated, but monster 111 wasn't defeated. So the order of defeating the monsters becomes {1}\{1\}{1}.
Case 444: Monster 111 wasn't defeated yet, but monster 333 didn't arrive yet. So the order of defeating the monsters becomes {1,3}\{1,3\}{1,3}.
Case 555: Monster 111 wasn't defeated yet, but monster 333 did arrive. So the order of defeating the monsters becomes {3,1}\{3,1\}{3,1}.

Hence, there are a total of 555 possible ways.
